back: Centralizes and automates backup policies (schedule, retention, cross-region/cross-account copy) across EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, and more from one place, instead of configuring native backup features per-service separately — the answer whenever a scenario wants one unified backup policy/compliance story across many resource types.

Network-attached block storage for EC2, tied to a single AZ (must snapshot to move across AZs — snapshots are stored in S3 behind the scenes).

Volume types matter on the exam: gp3 (general purpose baseline), io2/io2 Block Express (highest IOPS, for latency-sensitive/critical DBs), st1 (throughput-optimized HDD for big sequential workloads like big data), sc1 (cold HDD, cheapest, infrequent access).

Continuously replicates on-premises or cloud servers to a low-cost staging area in AWS, allowing fast (minutes) failover/recovery during a disaster — AWS's current recommended DR tool, effectively superseding the older CloudEndure Disaster Recovery branding.

Managed, fully elastic NFS file system that many EC2 instances (and Lambda functions) can mount concurrently across multiple AZs — unlike EBS, which attaches to only one instance at a time (except io2 Multi-Attach in narrow cases).

Exam trigger: 'shared file storage accessible by multiple Linux instances simultaneously' points to EFS, not EBS.

Family of managed third-party file systems: FSx for Windows File Server (SMB, AD-integrated, for Windows workloads), FSx for Lustre (high-performance parallel file system for HPC/ML, can link directly to S3 as a data repository), FSx for NetApp ONTAP, and FSx for OpenZFS.

Exam trigger: pick the specific FSx variant matching the workload's native file system requirement (e.g. Lustre for HPC/ML, Windows File Server for AD-joined Windows apps).

Object storage with 11 nines of durability, unlimited scale.

Storage classes trade cost for retrieval speed/frequency: Standard (frequent access), Standard-IA/One Zone-IA (infrequent access, cheaper storage + retrieval fee), Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves objects between tiers based on access patterns — no retrieval fee, ideal for unpredictable access), and the Glacier family for archival.

Lifecycle policies automate transitions/expirations — a very frequent exam cost-optimization pattern.

front: Amazon S3 Glacier (S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval / Deep Archive)
back: Lowest-cost long-term archival storage classes within S3.

Hybrid storage connecting on-premises applications to AWS storage via standard protocols: File Gateway (NFS/SMB backed by S3), Volume Gateway (iSCSI block volumes, cached or stored mode, backed by EBS snapshots in S3), and Tape Gateway (virtual tape library backed by S3/Glacier, for replacing physical backup tape infrastructure).

Exam trigger: 'on-premises application needs to use AWS storage without being rewritten' via a familiar protocol.

front: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval vs. Deep Archive — what's the retrieval tradeoff?
back:
Flexible Retrieval: minutes (Expedited) to hours (Standard/Bulk).

Deep Archive: cheapest tier, 12-hour standard retrieval — meant for compliance archives rarely if ever accessed.
